item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: could i get a clarifier please?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I regret to say I had to consult SCPDeclassified to have a clue what this was. Very interesting concept (always wanted to see the Foundation completely mess up containing an infohazard like this) but perhaps a little too esoteric in style.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… That's just a weaker 055 with a smarter format?

-1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The more that I think about it the more I enjoy it, which is kind of contrary if I'm honest. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck you. Fuck you. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm all for articles that force the reader to do some work. It allows for a more interactive experience. But there just isn't enough there to give much of a coherent story, or the necessary details are very obscure. Of course, despite challenging puzzles being less popular than easy/moderate ones, they still exist for a reason. There is an audience for it. Although I did not like it, I do think it fills a valuable niche.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed, it definitely does some interesting things here. I know we are critical of new format screws (as we should be), but this isn't really similar to other works linked in the forums.

I personally upvoted, but I can see why others wouldn't. I must say I didn't expect this work to be so controversial though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. It's a great way of showing the reader that something's wrong right off the bat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clever, solid concept, good execution, and doesn't overstay its welcome.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got wigged out by this more than the previous incarnation of the article did. While I don't fully get it and can see where others are coming from in their crit, the use of empty space paired with what details we have was nicely creepy.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If nothing else, it's impressive you were able to create such a polarizing piece. I was shocked when I saw how many people had rated this.

That aside, I felt you managed to pull off the 'nothing is scarier' trope pretty well, so plus one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit, this is a confusing one at first glance. There's not much here to get. All that's here is a skeleton of a containment protocol that paints a pretty grim picture for whichever iteration of the Foundation has to deal with this threat.

rambly thoughts & spoilers
– hide block

Altogether this SCP bears a strong resemblance to other insidious threats like SCP-3125, but it's still unique in its execution because it approaches such a threat in ways that 3125 doesn't. I don't even think it's fair to compare this one to 3125 since it uses a different way of "infecting" people, and the only concept it shares is that of the noosphere. But I can't deny that this reminded me of it.

The containment procedures here make sense, and I think they're actually pretty smart. This is what I like in particular:

  • Throwing up "unreality" bubbles that the predator will terminate itself on accidentally if it takes too many risks in its approach.
  • Giving affected personnel mnestics that eliminate their ability to forget, thereby starving the predator because it can't consume that which is indestructible.
    • I've never actually seen mnestics used in something that isn't antimemetic, and it's great that you found a way to execute that here.
  • Using cognitohazards to attack the predator, which it consumes before suffering the same effects a human would have, killing it.

There are a lot of cool things here and I like them a lot. I very much prefer reading SCPs where half the information isn't expunged or hidden because of some in-universe conceit, but you make a strong and compelling enough case here. +1 for sure

– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt like my brain was cronched like a cornflake and that's not a very comfortable mouthfeel +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At a certain point, I think an author should ask themself if the central conceit is too hard to "get". Essentially, what's the payoff, and what did I need to do to get there? In this case, it felt like the entire article was based around trying to figure out what this thing was, which might've been fine if it had some other neat things to offset the problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My thoughts are similar. I understand you can't really do the format screw with too much more buildup, at least not in the way I believe this is intended. I can't think of a way that would both do what's intended, as well as satisfy me, but like… I don't know. I don't feel invested enough, or have a proper context to feel anything from this.

item.title: Re: ???
item.content:encoded: People can have differing opinions than you on an article. Provided their opinions are constructive, you should at least try to appreciate and understand why they feel differently from you. It helps you appreciate works better and helps be able to form your opinions and critique skills (in any medium) more solidly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A very clever conceit, but the fact that you throw this singular thing at us with very little buildup or narrative backing it up makes the whole thing feel unearned. It's the kind of thing that would work extremely well as part of a larger whole, but doesn't quite work on its own for me.

Reluctant downvote. Might reconsider that later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty damn spot on for the first two parts.

re: horror media. contains spoiler
– hide block

the cerebrophagy is probably the most tenuous connection to what i was thinking, which is also why its the most visceral one; even without knowing concretely how it connects i wanted it to be unnerving enough to stand.

how it connects, though, is that its supposed to further clue one into the nature of the object. the act of eating implies an activity and an agency to it. this thing doesnt just destroy, it eats, and that means that there's some degree of life to it.

originally, this clue wasn't going to be on its own. when i was in drafting stages, i had

Item Type: Egovore

before the containment procedures to reinforce the "active eating" idea, but i decided against it because

  1. the article didn't need it, and
  2. preemptively avoiding some people objecting to me including that sort of thing in an already very screwy format screw >_>

item.title: Re: ???
item.content:encoded:
This is definitely not the "worst scp" in my opinion, this does have a concept/narrative that it's conveying if you look hard enough, and it's doing it in a very brave way. That's more than I can say for a lot of other articles.

Also, don't harass people because they like an article you don't.
what the hell did i just read? nice gif but that's not all a page is, in my opinion this is the worst scp i have read -1 anyone who upvoted that has SCP-4830 themselves

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It took me three reads, but I feel like I Got It™.

Spoiler!
I could be wrong lol

So it's a form of Kuru/Creutzfeldt-Jakob where the brain doesn't just destroy itself and the person's knowledge and memories, it destroys them for everyone? That's cool as all hell, especially with the format screw reflecting the destruction of tissue while being the literal destruction of information.

And the DC is to convince everyone it's just an outbreak of an airborne form of the regular CJD? The only bit I don't have a grasp on is the saturation of horror media with brain-eating, aside from that being how Kuru spreads.

Correct me if I'm wrong or missed something, but I really love this regardless! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No other description expungement skip has done what this does. The actual closest comparison this article has (and even then it'd be disingenuous to compare the two) might honestly be SCP-3005. The 2165 comparison is more logical, I feel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this was done better in SCP-579. The image is neat and there’s some cool imagery in here, but it doesn’t feel like there’s anything tying it all together.

In 579, as well as other SCPs in this meta, short-form genre (like SCP-2165), you can tell that there’s some central thread of logic running through the semi-coherent substance. Here, I couldn’t feel it. It felt almost completely detached to me.

I don’t know, maybe I have to be in the right mood to read it, like canary said above. But as it stands now, -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, sometimes I just appreciate a good "get in, get out" skip, and this one got me at just the right time, I guess! There's definitely something godawful happening, information and memory and consciousness is all super borked and the article barely has a chance to get started before it's over (in a good way!). Who knows what the hell is happening out there, but it's probably not great, and this I thought sold the pure nonsense chaos of that pretty well, and an extended, and more straightforward read wouldn't shove that overriding sense of helplessness at the viewer. I dunno, I dug it, +     well         sense             it
       
       
       
        over

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I die?

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: (psst i responded to your original post scroll up a little bit)

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: I don't get it. What is it even supposed to mean?
This is a format screw that is normally pretty awful, but you made it work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
spoilers maybe
– hide block

From what I can gather, this is some form of transition of thought to information, like raw information. Phrases like "Airborne Transmissible Spongiform Encephalopathy" lead me to believe that this "information" in our thoughts is physically emerging from people's heads from holes in their brains. The skull rupture part also leads me to believe this. This means that our memory is literally pouring out of our minds.

So why's the page fucked up? My current theory is that we are infected by whatever this is, and that it's meant to convey that we are slowly forgetting the page because of the anomaly itself. We are forgetting what the anomaly is because we are infected by it ourselves.

I'm probably wrong, but like, this is just what I've gathered so far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might be missing something, but that DESCRIPTION: should be lower-case. Otherwise, +1. Really like the image/gif.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get. Is there supposed to be something here? The trope of most content being erased means this is going to be a -1 for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm thoroughly unnerved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the header change is a really nice touch

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: uhh… i don't particularly understand this, but it's good enough for a +1 by my standards, a little short compared to other blanked out words scps if this was an approach for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be clear, here's the source for the image. Each individual 'frame' is CC 3.0 compliant (staff will have to click on the images to see the license).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't get it and either my formatting is messed up or I get it even less.
Im no-voting for the time being and will check in later to give proper criticism when I do get it (or have been spoiled in the comments).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Images © Nevit Dilmen ; originally they were individual, but assembled now in a gif for your pleasure. Edited by me and The Great Hippo.

this concept was originally, and will in the future, go somewhere else.

big big BIG thank you to The Great Hippo for more or less workshopping this with me. also thank you to Uncle Nicolini for critique.
